narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, an LSAT score of 31, upper-middle family income, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school deciles from Year 1 and Year 3. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated an 84.2% chance that you would fail the bar on your first attempt—far higher than what it sees in most applicants who get approved. This high predicted failure risk is why your application was rejected, as the school uses this tool to prioritize students likely to pass the bar and succeed in their program. The three biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your Year 1 decile, and your LSAT score, in that order of influence. Your Year 3 decile of 1—much lower than the typical student's 5.53—had the strongest negative impact, signaling to the model a significant drop in performance that often correlates with bar struggles among similar students. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 2, well below the average of 5.71, reinforced concerns about early academic challenges that didn't improve enough. Your LSAT score of 31, compared to the usual 36.7, further pushed the prediction toward failure by falling short of what the model associates with strong bar passers. While your GPA was right around the average of 3.21 and your full-time status is common for 93% of students, these weren't enough to offset the top concerns. In contrast to typical admitted students, who tend to have higher deciles and LSATs that predict bar passage, your profile stood out as riskier based on these key gaps. This doesn't reflect your full potential or personal story, but rather statistical patterns the model has learned. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying next cycle with updated achievements that address these areas. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by targeting these exact improvements, and schools value persistence.
